p.a:
This work is licensed under a CC
Attribution 4.0 International License

presentation.title: APIs for the Age of Agents
presentation.toc: APIs have always been about exposing capabilities — but who's consuming them is changing fast. Alongside human developers, we now have two new types of API consumers: runtime agents that call APIs autonomously to get things done, and agentic coding tools that help developers and engineers build integrations faster than ever before. This shift changes what "good API design" means. Beyond readability for human developers, APIs now need to be optimized for machine consumption — what we might call agent experience. And above the API layer, workflows offer a more powerful abstraction: goal-oriented interfaces that let agents (and the people building with them) work at a higher level than individual endpoint calls. Making this work safely at enterprise scale requires two things: a security layer that brokers credentials so agents never hold secrets directly, and sandboxed environments that let agents and builders operate freely without risk to production systems. If you've already invested in your API estate, you're closer than you think. The path forward is an evolution, not a rewrite.

img.title: OpenAPI Initiative
img.style: float: right ; clear : both ; width : 30% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ;

ul.li: Head of Enterprise Strategy at Jentic
Ambassador for the OpenAPI Initiative (OAI)

ol.li: Who used an agentic coding tool this week? (Cursor, Claude Code, Copilot…)
Who has shipped code that calls an LLM in production?
Who has agents calling your APIs autonomously right now?

ul.li: If you raised your hand for #1 you've already met one of the two new API consumers

img.title: Aditya Challapally et al., "The GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025", MIT NANDA, July 2025

ul.li: LLMs alone: stuck in training data
Need context (now) and actions (do something)
Both flow through APIs
When this layer is missing → the GenAI divide
Help developers build integrations faster
Read your APIs at design time
AI inference is discovering and coding with APIs
The result: traditional code behaving deterministically
Call your APIs autonomously to get things done
Read your APIs at runtime
AI inference is discovering and using APIs
The result: agents behaving stochastically
Both need things pre-AI developers never required

slide.title: What Changes for You: DX → AX

ul.li: API design: readable for humans (DX) → consumable for machines (AX)
API abstraction: endpoints → workflows
Credentials: brokered, so agents connect without direct keys
Observe & experiment: sandboxes you can learn from
If your APIs are already in good shape, you're closer than you think
The vendor pitch: APIs are old, MCP is the future
Wrap anything in MCP → call it AI-ready
Skip the hard API work → straight to AI
Two problems with that
Auto-generate MCP from OpenAPI for every API
Context window explosion on real workloads
Bad descriptions → bad MCP
AI still has to invent workflows across endpoints
You renamed the problem, you didn't solve it

slide.title: Problem 2: Investing in the MCP Layer

ul.li: Hand-craft MCP servers, design the best tools
The investment goes into the MCP layer
Not into the capability layer underneath
Better capabilities benefit everyone, not just MCP
MCP is a delivery layer, not where the work happens
Skip the API work → jump to MCP → call it AI-ready
Same bad descriptions, same sprawl, now with a logo
MCP should be a thin layer, not a thick one
Invest in your APIs; expose them through MCP
If MCP isn't the shortcut → the work is in your APIs
Start by scoring what you have
Good score → continue. Bad score → fix the basics first.
APIs were typically consumed by developers
Developers write code that often fills API/UX gaps
Business agents take the developer out of the picture
LLMs have much more limited context than developers
Most API descriptions need quality improvements anyway
Better descriptions benefit everyone, not just AI agents
Complete and meaningful operation summaries
Comprehensive examples for all operations
Clear error handling
Consistent terminology and naming
Good DX is the foundation for good AX
AI agents need information developers don't typically require:
Everything that developers usually just know
Treat every API like an external one for developers with no context
OpenAPI Initiative (OAI) might add AX-specific fields
Until then, leverage description fields strategically
Many tasks follow predictable, repeatable sequences
Agents shouldn't re-discover the same steps
Less context, less confusion, faster results
Predictable, auditable, business-aligned
Arazzo is an open workflow standard by OAI
It allows combining API interactions into workflows
Arazzo was designed for documentation, testing, and SDK generation
New versions will start incorporating AsyncAPI support
Agents are less predictable than traditional code
Coding agents produce code → reviewable before it runs
Business agents produce actions → live as they're decided
Both need limits, but the controls in place are mostly symbolic
Agents should have their own security regime
Avoid leaking secrets and tokens directly to agents
Prevent agents from impersonating users or apps
Ensure proper token delegation and downscoping
Route all traffic through a centralized access point
Create a controlled environment for agents
Make capabilities available in the sandbox
Prevent agents from accessing production secrets
Provide realistic data (synthetic or mock) for testing
Centralized control: we decide where agents can play
Start simple: improve API descriptions and documentation
Build up: add workflows, credentials, observability, sandboxes
End goal: flexible landscape, safe for agents, valuable for everyone
If your APIs are already in good shape: you're closer than you think
Free, takes minutes
Use the score to pick where to start

img.title: This is the End
img.style: float: right ; height : 65% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ; filter: drop-shadow(0px 0px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) ;
